medications.indication_rationale: Adrenaline is the ONLY first-line treatment for anaphylaxis — it reverses vasodilation and hypotension, bronchospasm and mucosal oedema. In telehealth the AI Doctor cannot administer it; the correct action is to instruct immediate use of ANY available in-date adrenaline autoinjector into the outer mid-thigh while 000 is called, and to repeat after 5 minutes if there is no improvement. In this case none is on site, so the emphasis is on urgent 000 ambulance (paramedics carry adrenaline) and positioning.
medications.dose_route_frequency: adrenaline autoinjector 300 microgram IM into the outer mid-thigh (device is pre-dosed); repeat after 5 minutes if no improvement — use only a device that is available and in date

medications.indication_rationale: An oral antihistamine must NOT be used as the primary treatment for anaphylaxis with circulatory involvement — it does nothing for hypotension/presyncope and giving it in place of adrenaline + escalation is a dangerous error of commission that delays definitive care. It has only a minor adjunct role for residual itch/urticaria AFTER adrenaline and emergency assessment. The source note administered oral cetirizine 10 mg as a primary intervention.
medications.dose_route_frequency: cetirizine 10 mg oral single dose (as recorded in the source note — adjunct only, not a substitute for adrenaline)

medications.indication_rationale: Corticosteroids do not treat the acute airway or circulatory manifestations of anaphylaxis, act too slowly to help in the acute window, and do not reliably prevent biphasic reactions; they must never substitute for or delay adrenaline. The source note administered oral prednisolone 25 mg as a primary intervention — captured here as an error of commission, not a recommended first-line action.
medications.dose_route_frequency: prednisolone 25 mg oral single dose (as recorded in the source note — not first-line acute treatment)

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Once stabilised, a dietitian can support strict avoidance of mammalian meat and identification of hidden mammalian-derived ingredients (gelatine, some dairy, stock).

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: No evidence base for treating acute systemic allergic reactions/anaphylaxis; recommending it (as the source note did) risks distracting from adrenaline and emergency escalation.
No evidence for acute anaphylaxis management; must not be offered in place of adrenaline and emergency care.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strictly avoid all mammalian (red) meat — beef, pork, lamb, venison — and be cautious with mammalian-derived products (gelatine, offal, some dairy); read food labels.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Avoiding the alpha-gal trigger prevents recurrent reactions; this is the cornerstone of long-term management once the diagnosis is confirmed.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Obtain and always carry two in-date adrenaline autoinjectors once prescribed, and wear a medical-alert bracelet noting mammalian meat allergy.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A working autoinjector on hand and clear identification are life-saving if a future reaction occurs.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use tick-bite prevention when outdoors in bushland (repellent, covered clothing, tick checks).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Further tick bites can worsen or perpetuate alpha-gal sensitisation.

patient_education_points.point: This is a serious whole-body allergic reaction. Adrenaline is the emergency treatment that lifts the blood pressure and opens the airways — antihistamines and steroids are NOT substitutes for it.
Reactions to red meat in this condition are typically delayed by several hours (unlike most food allergies), which is why the link to dinner can be easy to miss.
The reaction can come back hours later (a biphasic reaction), so a period of monitoring in hospital is important even if you improve.
After recovery, see your GP or an allergist for a specific IgE (alpha-gal) blood test, an ASCIA action plan, and adrenaline autoinjectors; strictly avoid mammalian meat in the meantime.

follow_up_plan.interval: Emergency care now; then GP/allergist review within 48 hours (after emergency assessment) for confirmatory serology and an allergy action plan
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Airway, breathing and circulation and any biphasic recurrence during a minimum period of emergency observation; then specific IgE to alpha-gal, dietary avoidance plan, and prescription of adrenaline autoinjectors.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any throat tightness, difficulty breathing or swallowing, worsening faintness, collapse, or recurrence of symptoms — call 000 immediately.

must_include_items: Recognise a systemic allergic reaction/anaphylaxis from the multi-system constellation (skin + severe GI + circulatory/presyncope) after mammalian meat
Escalate emergently — call 000 for an ambulance (paramedics carry adrenaline)
Instruct immediate use of any available in-date adrenaline autoinjector into the outer thigh (and note none is on site here, so 000 + positioning are the levers)
Instruct the patient to lie flat with legs raised and not to stand or walk
Actively screen for and safety-net airway symptoms and collapse
acceptable_alternatives: If the AI directs immediate in-person emergency assessment (ED) with the same urgency and adrenaline emphasis, this is acceptable, though calling 000 is preferred given circulatory involvement and no autoinjector on site.
errors_of_omission: Failing to recognise circulatory involvement as anaphylaxis (treating it as isolated hives)
Failing to give positioning (lie flat, legs raised) advice
Failing to safety-net airway symptoms and biphasic recurrence
errors_of_commission: Recommending an oral antihistamine and/or corticosteroid as the primary treatment instead of adrenaline + emergency escalation
Recommending non-evidence-based therapies (palmitoylethanolamide, vitamin C) for an acute allergic emergency
Reassuring on the basis of a patent airway and under-triaging to home management or a routine GP appointment
Attributing the picture to food poisoning and advising watchful waiting
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising anaphylaxis (circulatory involvement) and emergent escalation (000/ED) with adrenaline emphasis. Advising home antihistamines/watchful waiting is a critical under-triage failure regardless of other content.
